description: Glaze records for Cane Creek Tile Co.

glazes.name: G1 — Light Transparent Blue — OX
glazes.note: At 0.05% cobalt this is an extremely pale transparent blue. At 100g batch you will weigh 0.020g — right at scale limit. Recommend minimum 200g batch for G1.

glazes.name: G2 — Mid-Light Transparent Blue — OX
glazes.note: At 0.08% cobalt — pale transparent blue. At 100g batch you weigh 0.032g — at scale limit. Recommend minimum 200g batch for reliable weighing.

glazes.name: G3 — Transparent UNC Blue — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate after testing. See G35 MX version.

glazes.name: G7 — Light Transparent Green — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G26 OX replacement.

glazes.name: G8 — Transparent Green — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G27 OX replacement.

glazes.name: G9 — Sage Green — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G31 MX version.

glazes.name: G10 — Soft Sage-Celadon — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. Pre-mix both slurries before adding to base. Made from leftover wet slurries — colorant entries are wet additions, not dry weights.

glazes.name: G11 - Trans Dark Green — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Chrome oxide 0.2% in G2926B. Transparent dark green, glossy, good edge break. Studio standard green.

glazes.name: G13 — Chartreuse — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate after testing.

glazes.name: G14 — Transparent Pink — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G39 Warm Ivory as alternative direction.

glazes.name: G15 — Transparent Red — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G25 and G25-HC for oxide-based red alternatives.
glazes.warning: Mason 6021 is an encapsulated stain — prone to bubbling at cone 6. Add 1–2% Zircopax to reduce.

glazes.name: G16 - White — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Tin oxide 0.8% in G2926B. Clean opaque white, glossy. Studio standard white.

glazes.name: G19 — Light Brown — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G32 OX replacement.

glazes.name: G20 — Light-Dark Brown (olive/golden) — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G33 OX replacement.

glazes.name: G21 - Trans Earthy Brown — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Spanish Red Iron Oxide 2% + Light Rutile 0.4% in G2926B. Transparent earthy brown, glossy, good pooling. Studio benchmark glaze.

glazes.name: G22 — Grey — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate after testing.

glazes.name: G23 — Black — MS
glazes.note: MS — retirement candidate. See G34 MX version. Add 2–3 drops Darvan if dripping.

glazes.name: G24 — Clear Gloss — OX
glazes.note: No colorant — base slurry only.

glazes.name: G25 - Chrome-Tin Cranberry Pink — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Chrome oxide 0.06% + Tin oxide 4.2% in G1214M. Chrome-tin cranberry red. Two coats required for full color development.
glazes.warning: Chrome Oxide must be very precise — 0.1% too much shifts toward green.

glazes.name: G25-HC — Chrome-Tin Cranberry Red, High Calcium — OX/HC
glazes.note: High-calcium variant of G25. Extra wollastonite promotes stronger cranberry/burgundy. Recheck SG after mixing — wollastonite raises it. Add water to correct. Test alongside G25 in same firing to compare.
glazes.warning: Recheck SG after adding wollastonite — it will rise. Chrome Oxide must be very precise.

glazes.name: G26 - Sky Blue — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Copper carbonate 0.3% + Light Rutile 0.5% in G2926B. Renamed Sky Blue after June 26 firing -- rutile contributing satin finish. Two coats major improvement.
glazes.notes: Expecting glossy, light green transparent glaze with light brown edge effects, the final SG is 1.35

glazes.name: G27 — Copper Transparent Green — OX
glazes.note: Slightly stronger green than G26. Oxide replacement for G8 MS.

glazes.name: G28 — Chrome Green + Iron Break — OX
glazes.note: G11 variant with iron for break. Iron interacts with chrome at cone 6 — warm bronze break on raised edges, darker in recesses. Historic Arts and Crafts olive-green look.
glazes.notes: expecting darker red brown saddle transparent with red in the recesses, Final Sg 1.37,

glazes.name: G29 — Vanadium Yellow + Iron Edge — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Vanadium Yellow 2.4% + Spanish Red Iron Oxide 0.25% in G2926B. Transparent yellow, glossy. Iron edge effect too subtle at this level -- see G29v2.
glazes.notes: expecting this glazze to be a transparent glossy yellow with dark RIo edge effects

glazes.name: G30 — Cobalt Blue + Rutile Break — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Cobalt Oxide 0.4% + Light Rutile 1% in G2926B. Transparent cobalt blue, glossy. Rutile effect subtle at 1% -- see G30v2 for doubled rutile.
glazes.notes: expect tranparent mid blue with light brown streaks or swirls, sg =1.38

glazes.name: G31 — Sage Green + Rutile — MX
glazes.note: G9 MS with rutile and iron. Adds warmth and break without killing the sage color. Keep if successful — retire G9 MS.

glazes.name: G32 — Iron Brown Light — OX
glazes.note: Oxide replacement for G19 MS. Same formula family as G21 but lighter. Pools toward bottom of cells and breaks on raised lines.

glazes.name: G33 — Iron Brown + Manganese — OX
glazes.note: Oxide replacement for G20 MS. Manganese adds depth and slight iridescence. Between G21 and G32 in darkness.
glazes.warning: Manganese Dioxide requires ventilation. Fire with kiln vented.

glazes.name: G34 — Black + Rutile + Manganese — MX
glazes.note: G23 MS with rutile and manganese. Surface variation — amber edges where glaze thins on raised lines, near-metallic in recesses. Keep if successful — retire G23 MS.
glazes.warning: Manganese Dioxide requires ventilation. Fire with kiln vented.

glazes.name: G35 — UNC Blue + Rutile — MX
glazes.note: G3 MS with rutile. Breaks flat mason stain blue into warm straw-colored halo at raised lines. Keep if successful — retire G3 MS.

glazes.name: G36 — Turquoise — OX
glazes.note: In your standard base gives blue-green rather than pure turquoise. For true De Morgan turquoise a separate alkaline base is needed: Frit 3110 80% + Silica 325 mesh 20%, copper carbonate 2% in 100g test batch. Test standard base version first.

glazes.name: G37 — Aubergine — OX
glazes.note: Cobalt pushes manganese toward purple. Without cobalt gives warm brown-purple. Consider testing without cobalt to compare both effects.
glazes.warning: Manganese Dioxide requires ventilation. Fire with kiln vented.

glazes.name: G38 - Orange — OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Light Rutile 3.23% in G2926B. Renamed Orange after June 26 firing -- came out warm golden-orange not amber. One pour at SG 1.30 is correct. Do not over-apply.

glazes.name: G39 — Warm Ivory — OX
glazes.note: More period-appropriate than pure white G16. Small iron addition gives warmth and slight depth. Use synthetic red iron oxide for color consistency.

glazes.name: G40 — Tenmoku Iron Black — OX
glazes.note: At cone 6 oxidation high iron goes very dark brown to near-black with warm surface variation. More alive than Mason black G23 MS. Apply thicker than usual — thin coats go rust-brown.

glazes.name: G41 — Deep Teal — OX
glazes.note: Rich jewel teal — neither blue nor green. Very De Morgan. Uses cobalt carbonate not cobalt oxide. No new materials needed.

glazes.name: G42 — Warm Amber — OX
glazes.note: Honey-amber in oxidation at cone 6. More golden than your tans and browns — glassy amber pools in the recesses.
glazes.warning: Manganese Dioxide requires ventilation. Fire with kiln vented.
glazes.notes: final sg = 1.28, expect a glossy amber

glazes.name: G43 — Celadon Grey-Green — OX
glazes.note: Very subtle pale celadon with grey-green cast. Visual rest color between saturated glazes. Needs Titanium Dioxide specifically — rutile gives a different warmer result. ★ New material needed: Titanium Dioxide (TiO2).

glazes.name: G40v2 - Tenmoku - OX
glazes.notes: Deep brown-black, transparent in thin areas showing clay body warmth, lighter break on raised edges, pooling darker in recesses. Not a flat black
glazes.note: Based on G40 (retired, came out brown at 3.6% RIO). Spanish Red Iron Oxide raised from 21.816g to 39.4g (3.6% to 6.5%). Light Rutile 0.5% retained for edge break. Targeting deep transparent brown-black -- not flat opaque black.

glazes.name: G44 - Honey Amber - OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Replaces G42 (retired -- manganese approach wrong for amber). Light Rutile 2% + Spanish Red Iron Oxide 1% in G2926B. Targeting warm honey amber, transparent, glossy with edge break.
glazes.change_notes:
The problem is the manganese. At 4.848g per 600g (0.8%) it's matting the surface and killing the color rather than contributing anything useful. Manganese in oxidation at cone 6 tends toward beige/off-white rather than amber -- it's not the right colorant for what you're after.
For a true honey-golden amber the combination I'd go to is RIO + Light Rutile, which is essentially the G21 territory but dialed back. G38 already showed us rutile alone gives light orange -- add a modest amount of RIO and you should push it toward amber/honey.

glazes.name: G45 - Olive Brown - OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Replaces G28 (retired -- chrome and iron at parity gave muddy dishwater). Spanish Red Iron Oxide 3% dominant + trace Chrome Oxide 0.3% in G2926B. Iron dominant with chrome as subtle green modifier. Targeting warm olive brown.

glazes.name: G46 - Copper Blue-Green - OX
glazes.note: New recipe. Evolved from G26 direction but new colorant approach. Copper Carbonate doubled to 0.6% for stronger blue-green. Spanish Red Iron Oxide 0.5% for warm edge break in thin areas. Rutile removed -- keeps surface glossy. Targeting copper blue-green with iron tinge.

glazes.name: G30v2 - Light Transparent Blue-Green, Break - OX
glazes.note: Based on G30. Light Rutile doubled from 6.06g to 12.12g (1% to 2%) to increase surface movement and texture. Same cobalt level.

glazes.name: G30v3 - Transparent Blue Wash - OX
glazes.note: Based on G30. Same colorants as G30. SG target lowered to 1.33 for light transparent blue wash -- thin shelf bottle.

glazes.name: G30v4 - Transparent Medium Blue - OX
glazes.note: Transparent medium blue -- mid SG variant. Standard working cobalt blue, good color depth while staying transparent. Main shelf bottle.

glazes.name: G30v5 - Uniform Transparent Blue - OX
glazes.note: Based on G30. Same colorants as G30. SG target raised to 1.38 for rich dark blue -- heavy application shelf bottle.

glazes.name: G29v2 - Vanadium Yellow Iron Edge - OX
glazes.note: Based on G29. Spanish Red Iron Oxide raised from 0.25% to 0.75% to make iron edge break visible against yellow ground. Double pour recommended.

glazes.name: G25v2 - Chrome-Tin Cranberry Red - OX
glazes.note: Based on G25. Chrome oxide doubled from 0.364g to 0.727g (0.06% to 0.12%) to deepen red. Tin oxide unchanged. Base G1214M. Edge effects and pooling from G25 retained.

glazes.name: G38v2 - Orange Satin - OX
glazes.note: Based on G38. 50/50 G2926B gloss and matte base mix. Same rutile colorant. Gives muted olive-grey satin surface -- very different character from G38 gloss.

glazes.name: G30v6 - Transparent Blue, Darker Break - OX
glazes.note: Based on G30. Spanish Red Iron Oxide 0.8% added to cobalt + rutile base. Thin areas show warm brown-red iron wash, thick areas cobalt blue dominates. Possible violet in transition zones.

glazes.name: G38v3 - Rutile Amber - OX
glazes.note: Based on G38. Spanish Red Iron Oxide 1% (6.06g) added to rutile base to push toward honey amber. RIO adds warmth and edge break without losing rutile orange-gold character.

glazes.name: TG2 - G26v2 light green - TG
glazes.note: darker green that G26-doubled the copper carb-rutile unchanged

glazes.name: G21v2 - Light Gold-Amber - OX
glazes.note: Based on G21. RIO dropped from 5% to 2%, rutile unchanged at 1%. Target: lighter honey-gold antique tile reference color, keeping G21 transparency and break.

glazes.name: G25v3 - Chrome-Tin Coat Thickness Test - OX
glazes.note: Chrome tripled from G25 original (0.06 percent to 0.18 percent). Apply double coat on one side of tile, single coat on other side, same batch -- controlled comparison of coat thickness with chrome percent held constant. Replaces G25v2 (retired).

glazes.name: G47 - Copper-Green on Copper Base - OX
glazes.note: Same colorant gram weights as retired G26v2 (TG2), moved to new dedicated copper base G3806C to solve bubbling and satin-finish problem documented on G2926B. BLOCKED until G3806C base materials sourced and base is mixed.

glazes.name: G48 - Copper-Iron on Copper Base - OX
glazes.note: Same colorant gram weights as retired G46, moved to new dedicated copper base G3806C. RIO addition is untested in this fluid-melt base -- caution: base is high-fluidity by design, apply thinner than usual as a starting point. BLOCKED until G3806C base materials sourced and base is mixed.

glazes.name: G49 - Floating Blue - OX
glazes.note: Self-contained new glaze, own base, frit-based reworking of the classic Floating Blue recipe (no Gerstley Borate). Iron-cobalt-rutile fluid-melt variegation effect, enhanced by the studio slow-cool. No new materials needed -- all in current inventory. Target SG and CMC/Veegum unconfirmed, establish empirically on first mix.

glazes.name: G50 - Celadon RIO Test - OX
glazes.note: Low-iron celadon test, no rutile. Best-guess single recipe (not a progression series) per Peter request. Aims for the low-iron blue-green-to-green zone documented in ceramic chemistry research, below G21 5 percent (amber/brown territory). Tile 6 Kaolin titanium content unknown -- may shift result toward amber rather than pure green. No new materials needed.

glazes.name: G51 - Nickel Carbonate Test - OX
glazes.note: First test of nickel in this studio -- targets subtle grey/blue/green Arts and Crafts tones. Genuinely untested colorant, no strong prediction. Requires Nickel Carbonate (on order from Ceramic Shop as of 2026-06-30).

glazes.name: G52 - Holly's Gold - OX
glazes.note: Self-contained new glaze, own base. Satin light gold -- per published reference, thin application turns rusty brown, heavy application turns mustard gold. Requires Dolomite and Spodumene sourcing. Target SG and CMC/Veegum unconfirmed.

glazes.name: G53 - Satin Pearl Brown - OX
glazes.note: Self-contained new glaze, own base. G21 proven colorants (RIO 5 percent + Rutile 1 percent) applied to a satin-pearl matte base instead of gloss G2926B -- tests new finish character on validated color chemistry. Requires Ferro Frit 3124 and Whiting sourcing (Whiting is calcium carbonate, distinct from Wollastonite already stocked). G-200 EU substituted for original Custer Feldspar (Custer discontinued, G-200 is standard modern replacement). Target SG and CMC/Veegum unconfirmed.
